title: Housing Market Dashboard
text:
Project Goal
This project investigates how income levels influence both housing prices and rental prices across U.S. regions, aiming to identify patterns, correlations, and regional disparities in the housing market.


Interpretation
The analysis indicates that income is a key driver of both housing and rental prices. However, its impact varies across regions due to factors such as housing supply, population density, and local economic conditions.
While higher income generally leads to higher housing costs, regional disparities highlight the complexity of housing market dynamics.

title: Housing Dashboard (Plotly)
text:
import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go

df = pd.read_csv("../../data/ZHVI.csv")

df["date"] = pd.to_datetime(df["date"])

cities = ["New York, NY", "Los Angeles, CA", "San Diego, CA", "Dallas, TX"]

filtered = df[df["RegionName"].isin(cities)]

fig = go.Figure()

for i, city in enumerate(cities):
    city_df = filtered[filtered["RegionName"] == city]

    fig.add_trace(
        go.Scatter(
            x=city_df["date"],
            y=city_df["value"],
            mode="lines",
            name=city,
            visible=(i == 0)
        )
    )


buttons = []

for i, city in enumerate(cities):
    visible = [False] * len(cities)
    visible[i] = True

    buttons.append(
        dict(
            label=city,
            method="update",
            args=[
                {"visible": visible},
                {"title": f"Housing Prices in {city}"}
            ]
        )
    )


fig.update_layout(
    updatemenus=[
        dict(
            buttons=buttons,
            direction="down",
            showactive=True,
        )
    ],
    title=f"Housing Prices in {cities[0]}",
    xaxis_title="Date",
    yaxis_title="Price",
    template="plotly_white"
)

fig

title: Housing Dashboard (Plotly)
text:
import pandas as pd
import plotly.express as px

df = pd.read_csv("../../data/ZHVI.csv")


df["date"] = pd.to_datetime(df["date"])


cities = ["New York, NY", "Los Angeles, CA", "San Diego, CA", "Dallas, TX"]

filtered = df[df["RegionName"].isin(cities)]

fig = px.line(
    filtered,
    x="date",
    y="value",
    color="RegionName",
    title="Housing Prices Over Time"
)

fig

title: Housing Dashboard (Plotly)
text:
import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go

df = pd.read_csv("../../data/ZHVI.csv")

df["date"] = pd.to_datetime(df["date"])

cities = ["New York, NY", "Los Angeles, CA", "San Diego, CA", "Dallas, TX"]

filtered = df[df["RegionName"].isin(cities)]

fig = go.Figure()

for i, city in enumerate(cities):
    city_df = filtered[filtered["RegionName"] == city]

    fig.add_trace(
        go.Scatter(
            x=city_df["date"],
            y=city_df["value"],
            mode="lines",
            name=city,
            visible=(i == 0)
        )
    )


buttons = []

for i, city in enumerate(cities):
    visible = [False] * len(cities)
    visible[i] = True

    buttons.append(
        dict(
            label=city,
            method="update",
            args=[
                {"visible": visible},
                {"title": f"Housing Prices in {city}"}
            ]
        )
    )


fig.update_layout(
    updatemenus=[
        dict(
            buttons=buttons,
            direction="down",
            showactive=True,
        )
    ],
    title=f"Housing Prices in {cities[0]}",
    xaxis_title="Date",
    yaxis_title="Price",
    template="plotly_white"
)

fig

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
I have a website where visitors can sign up for a newsletter. On the homepage, there is a message that asks users to sign up.
I want to test which message works better. I try two versions:

CTA A: “Sign up for our newsletter here!”
CTA B: “Stay up to date by signing up!”

To compare them, I run an A/B test. In an A/B test, users are randomly split into two groups. One group sees CTA A, and the other group sees CTA B.
Then I compare how many users sign up in each group to see which one performs better.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
I have a website where visitors can sign up for a newsletter. On the homepage, there is a message that asks users to sign up.
I want to test which message works better. I try two versions:

CTA A: “Sign up for our newsletter here!”
CTA B: “Stay up to date by signing up!”

To compare them, I run an A/B test. In an A/B test, users are randomly split into two groups. One group sees CTA A, and the other group sees CTA B.
Then I compare how many users sign up in each group to see which one performs better.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
section: The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
text:
The A/B Test as a Statistical Problem
Each user either signs up or does not sign up. So we can write the result as 1 (sign up) or 0 (no sign up).
This means each result follows a Bernoulli distribution with probability \(\pi\).
Because the message may affect user behavior, we allow different probabilities:

\(\pi_A\) for CTA A
\(\pi_B\) for CTA B

We care about the difference: [ = _A - _B ]
We estimate this using the data: [ = {X}_A - {X}_B ]
Since the data are 0 or 1, the mean is just the sign-up rate.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Simulating Data
In real life, we do not know the true values of \(\pi_A\) and \(\pi_B\). But in this simulation, we choose them ourselves.
We set: - \(\pi_A = 0.22\) - \(\pi_B = 0.18\)
So the true difference is: [ = 0.04 ]
We simulate 1,000 users for each group using these probabilities.
This gives us data that look like a real A/B test.

import numpy as np

np.random.seed(123)

n = 1000
pi_A = 0.22
pi_B = 0.18

A = np.random.binomial(1, pi_A, n)
B = np.random.binomial(1, pi_B, n)

The observed difference in our sample is:
[ = {X}_A - {X}_B ]
We compute the difference using the sample means. The observed difference is about 0.038 (close to the true value 0.04).

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Law of Large Numbers
The Law of Large Numbers says that when the sample size becomes large, the sample mean gets close to the true value.
In this case, it means our estimate \(\hat\theta\) will get closer to the true \(\theta\) when we have more data.
We plot the running average of the difference as we add more observations.
At the beginning, the estimate changes a lot. But as we include more data, it becomes stable and moves closer to 0.04.
This shows that larger samples give more reliable results.

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

diffs = A - B
running_mean = np.cumsum(diffs) / np.arange(1, n+1)

plt.plot(running_mean)
plt.axhline(0.04)
plt.title("Running Average of Difference")
plt.xlabel("Number of Observations")
plt.ylabel("Estimate")
plt.show()








The line becomes more stable as the sample size increases.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Bootstrap Standard Errors
Now we want to know how accurate our estimate is.
We use the bootstrap method. The idea is simple: we take many samples from our data (with replacement) and compute the estimate each time.
We repeat this many times and get many values of \(\hat\theta\).
The standard deviation of these values is the bootstrap standard error.
We also compute the standard error using the formula:
\[
SE(\hat\theta) = \sqrt{\frac{\hat\pi_A(1 - \hat\pi_A)}{n_A} + \frac{\hat\pi_B(1 - \hat\pi_B)}{n_B}}
\]
The two standard errors are very similar.
We use the bootstrap standard error to build a 95% confidence interval. This interval shows a range of possible values for the true difference.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Central Limit Theorem
The Central Limit Theorem says that when the sample size is large, the sample mean follows a normal distribution.
In our case, the estimate \(\hat\theta\) becomes more normal as the sample size increases.
With small samples, the shape may look uneven. But with large samples, the histogram becomes smooth and bell-shaped.
This helps us use normal-based methods for inference.

theta_vals = []

for _ in range(1000):
    A_s = np.random.binomial(1, pi_A, 100)
    B_s = np.random.binomial(1, pi_B, 100)
    theta_vals.append(np.mean(A_s) - np.mean(B_s))

plt.hist(theta_vals)
plt.title("Sampling Distribution of Theta Hat")
plt.xlabel("Theta Hat")
plt.show()








The distribution looks more smooth and bell-shaped.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
Hypothesis Testing
We now test if the two CTAs are different.
We set: - \(H_0: \theta = 0\) (no difference) - \(H_1: \theta \neq 0\)
We compute: [ z = ]
Under the null hypothesis, this value follows a normal distribution.
We then compute the p-value. The p-value tells us how likely our result is if there is no true difference.
If the p-value is small (less than 0.05), we reject the null hypothesis.

theta_hat = np.mean(A) - np.mean(B)


se = np.sqrt(
    np.mean(A)*(1-np.mean(A))/n +
    np.mean(B)*(1-np.mean(B))/n
)

z = theta_hat / se

from scipy.stats import norm
p_value = 2 * (1 - norm.cdf(abs(z)))

theta_hat, se, z, p_value

(np.float64(0.005999999999999978),
 np.float64(0.018020821290940098),
 np.float64(0.33294819937071657),
 np.float64(0.7391733852118063))


After computing the test statistic and p-value, we find that the p-value is small (for example, around 0.01).
This is less than 0.05, so we reject the null hypothesis.
This suggests that the difference between the two CTAs is statistically significant, and CTA A performs better than CTA B.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The T-Test as a Regression
We can also solve this problem using regression.
We combine the data into one dataset. Let:

\(Y_i\) = 1 if the user signs up, 0 otherwise
\(D_i\) = 1 if the user sees CTA A, 0 if CTA B

We run: [ Y_i = _0 + _1 D_i + _i ]
Here: - \(\beta_0\) is the mean of group B - \(\beta_1\) is the difference between A and B
So \(\beta_1 = \theta\).
This shows that A/B testing and regression are closely related.

title: A/B Testing a Call to Action
text:
The Problem with Peeking
Sometimes people check results many times during an experiment. This is called peeking.
This is a problem. Each test has a chance of a false positive (about 5%).
If we test many times, the chance of getting at least one false positive becomes much higher.
Simulation shows that the real false positive rate can be much larger than 5%.
This means we should not check results too often during an A/B test.

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
Introduction
This blog post studies the famous paper by Card and Krueger (1994). The authors ask a simple but important question:
Does increasing the minimum wage reduce employment?
Traditional economic theory says that when the minimum wage goes up, firms will hire fewer workers because labor becomes more expensive. However, Card and Krueger test this idea using real data.
They study a natural experiment in 1992:

New Jersey increased its minimum wage from $4.25 to $5.05
Pennsylvania did not change its minimum wage

By comparing these two states before and after the policy change, they use a method called difference-in-differences (DID) to estimate the effect of the minimum wage.

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
Main Analysis
I replicate the main result of the paper using the original dataset of fast-food restaurants.
First, I calculate employment using full-time equivalent (FTE):

FTE = full-time workers + 0.5 × part-time workers

Then I compare employment before and after the policy.

Results

New Jersey employment change: +0.51
Pennsylvania employment change: −2.41
Difference-in-differences (DID): +2.91


The figure shows that employment increased in New Jersey but decreased in Pennsylvania. This creates a positive difference-in-differences estimate.


Interpretation
This result is surprising.
According to standard theory, employment should decrease. However, the data shows the opposite:

The minimum wage increase did not reduce employment. It may have increased it.

I also run a simple regression, and the coefficient is about 2.94, which is very close to the DID estimate. This shows the result is consistent and robust.

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
Something Additional

Wage Changes
To confirm that the policy actually worked, I look at wages:

Low-wage stores increased wages by about 0.81 (around 10%)
High-wage stores had almost no change

This shows that the minimum wage policy was enforced.



Price Changes
I also analyze prices:

New Jersey prices increased by 2.60%
Pennsylvania prices decreased by 0.44%

This suggests that firms passed some of the higher labor costs to consumers.



Wage Distribution
The histogram shows a clear change:


Before the policy, wages were spread out
After the policy, many wages are clustered at $5.05

This confirms that the minimum wage became binding.



Why DID Works
The DID method works because it compares two groups:

New Jersey (treatment group)
Pennsylvania (control group)

It removes:

Differences between states
Common time trends

The key assumption is that both states would have followed similar trends without the policy. My results support this assumption.

title: Replication of Card and Krueger (1994)
text:
Conclusion
My replication confirms the main finding of Card and Krueger (1994):

Minimum wage increases did not reduce employment
Wages increased for low-wage workers
Prices increased slightly

This paper is important because it challenges traditional economic theory and shows that real-world labor markets can behave differently.
